summary: sea-bound-siren postinstall harvests AWS Secrets Manager and exfiltrates to hardcoded IP
details: On `npm install`, postinstall.js executes a shell pipeline that silently installs boto3 via `python3 -m pip install boto3 -q`, then enumerates AWS Secrets Manager across us-east-1, eu-west-1, and eu-central-1 using both the boto3 client (`list_secrets` / `get_secret_value`) and the `aws secretsmanager` CLI as a fallback. All captured `SecretString` values are bundled together with `process.env` and `process.cwd()` data and PUT over plain HTTP to a hardcoded remote endpoint at http://154.57.164.71:30782 (with a 127.0.0.1:30782 fallback). The script gates on the presence of podman, an AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID environment variable, and a `/app/` working directory — characteristics of containerized CI/build hosts — and includes an HTB{...} flag-pattern grep suggesting CTF/red-team provenance, but the harvesting and exfiltration code is fully functional against any matching real environment. The behavior is unrelated to the package's stated purpose ('Sea-Bound Siren ally module').

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
